tweets.text:
The stronger cap ex intentions doesn't mean CEO's aren't worried.  

But unlike fund managers they're less worried about a credit and more worried about a cyber event which was their top tail risk at 65% (up from 55% in Q1) overtaking "Geopolitical" which edged up to 62%. 

Pandemics, climate, and social risks all garnered 16% or less in votes.

tweets.text:
Goldman: Despite the sharp rise in oil prices, company commentary and results suggest that the consumer remained healthy in the first quarter. 

Sales growth among consumer-facing companies improved—sales increased 9% year-over-year among the median S&P 500 consumer discretionary company and 5% for the median consumer staples company. 

That message from company results is corroborated by the official statistics and alternative data: Exhibit 2 shows that monthly measures of nominal consumer spending—including those that exclude gasoline purchases—have continued to grow at roughly the same rate for the last few months.
tweets.full_text:
Goldman: Despite the sharp rise in oil prices, company commentary and results suggest that the consumer remained healthy in the first quarter. 

Sales growth among consumer-facing companies improved—sales increased 9% year-over-year among the median S&P 500 consumer discretionary company and 5% for the median consumer staples company. 

That message from company results is corroborated by the official statistics and alternative data: Exhibit 2 shows that monthly measures of nominal consumer spending—including those that exclude gasoline purchases—have continued to grow at roughly the same rate for the last few months.

tweets.text:
I mentioned yesterday the record increase in job openings in professional/business services in April's JOLTS report, but Ed Yardeni adds an additional nuance I didn't realize:

"The composition of the April increase in job openings is striking. A 69% m/m surge in openings at establishments with 1-9 employees was the strongest on record.

"We think these ... facts could be connected to AI, which may be stimulating the formation of new AI-driven businesses, as evidenced by the ongoing rise in new business applications (chart)."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Gain in job openings dominated by professional and business services which saw openings surge +668k, easily a record (the next highest was Sept 2022 at 352k). https://t.co/eoDg3KkTSz

tweets.text:
Some info on the new proposed tariffs:

- To be applied under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 to 34 goods from 60 countries made with inputs produced with "forced labor."  Those included cotton used for garments, critical minerals for solar products, fish used for fish oil and fish meal, and palm fruit used palm oil.  Some apparel and textile imports though will get a reduced tariff according to the volume of US exports of textiles to those nations, and other products are exempt from the tariffs entirely, including beef, tomatoes, bananas, coffee, orange juice and other food items. Metals, which are already covered by other levies, are excluded, as are certain fuels and chemicals.
- 10% tariff applies to countries that impose prohibitions on forced labor imports or have committed to doing so including Canada, Mexico, the European Union, Taiwan and the UK.
- 12.5% tariff applies to those countries “that have failed to impose and effectively enforce” such prohibitions including China, India, Japan, South Korea, Brazil and Switzerland.
- The levies won’t go into effect immediately and are subject to a public comment and review period before implementation, which could result in changes before any duties are codified. Written comments are due to be submitted by July 6, and a Section 301 panel is expected to convene public hearings beginning on July 7, according to the notice.
- Section 301 tariffs are seen as more legally sound than many other tariff avenues, but also more time-consuming. The Hinrich Foundation’s Elms said the imposition of the newly announced tariffs is likely to coincide with the expiry of the existing Section 122 10% universal tariffs, once consultation and hearings wrap up.
- A separate batch of 301 investigations is expected to be released soon including a review of US trading partners’ excess manufacturing capacity.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
🇺🇸🌎 US Proposes Levies of at Least 10% as Trump Rebuilds Tariff Wall – Bloomberg
https://t.co/GRSKgsBoWi
tweets.full_text:
Some info on the new proposed tariffs:

- To be applied under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 to 34 goods from 60 countries made with inputs produced with "forced labor."  Those included cotton used for garments, critical minerals for solar products, fish used for fish oil and fish meal, and palm fruit used palm oil.  Some apparel and textile imports though will get a reduced tariff according to the volume of US exports of textiles to those nations, and other products are exempt from the tariffs entirely, including beef, tomatoes, bananas, coffee, orange juice and other food items. Metals, which are already covered by other levies, are excluded, as are certain fuels and chemicals.
- 10% tariff applies to countries that impose prohibitions on forced labor imports or have committed to doing so including Canada, Mexico, the European Union, Taiwan and the UK.
- 12.5% tariff applies to those countries “that have failed to impose and effectively enforce” such prohibitions including China, India, Japan, South Korea, Brazil and Switzerland.
- The levies won’t go into effect immediately and are subject to a public comment and review period before implementation, which could result in changes before any duties are codified. Written comments are due to be submitted by July 6, and a Section 301 panel is expected to convene public hearings beginning on July 7, according to the notice.
- Section 301 tariffs are seen as more legally sound than many other tariff avenues, but also more time-consuming. The Hinrich Foundation’s Elms said the imposition of the newly announced tariffs is likely to coincide with the expiry of the existing Section 122 10% universal tariffs, once consultation and hearings wrap up.
- A separate batch of 301 investigations is expected to be released soon including a review of US trading partners’ excess manufacturing capacity.

tweets.text:
BoA: "A silver lining in the ugly inflation data... The economy isn’t overheating"

"Although core PCE inflation has surged to 3.3% y/y, the Fed can take some solace from  the fact that underlying demand doesn’t seem to be accelerating. 

"For one, the labor market is still a bit soft and isn’t a source of inflationary pressure, as former Chair Powell often noted. For another, our analysis suggests that demand drivers of core PCE inflation have returned to pre-pandemic levels. 

"Inflation is elevated due to supply and 'ambiguous' drivers, which are a mix of supply and demand.  

"So, the Fed doesn’t need to rush to hike rates  Tepid demand gives the Fed more leeway to stay on hold. We think policymakers will continue to hold out hope that the ongoing supply disruptions are one-offs, unless a tightening labor market forces their hand to hike."
tweets.full_text:

"For one, the labor market is still a bit soft and isn’t a source of inflationary pressure, as former Chair Powell often noted. For another, our analysis suggests that demand drivers of core PCE inflation have returned to pre-pandemic levels. 

"Inflation is elevated due to supply and 'ambiguous' drivers, which are a mix of supply and demand.  

"So, the Fed doesn’t need to rush to hike rates  Tepid demand gives the Fed more leeway to stay on hold. We think policymakers will continue to hold out hope that the ongoing supply disruptions are one-offs, unless a tightening labor market forces their hand to hike."
